q: What is the difference between REST, gRPC, and GraphQL?
a: REST uses HTTP/1.1 with JSON and resource URLs; it is simple, cacheable, and the safe default for public APIs. gRPC uses HTTP/2 with binary Protobuf and a .proto contract that generates client/server stubs; it is fast, supports streaming, and is favored for internal service-to-service calls. GraphQL exposes a single endpoint with a typed schema and lets clients ask for exactly the fields they need, avoiding over/under-fetching. Pick REST for broad compatibility, gRPC for low-latency internal microservices, GraphQL for flexible client-driven queries over complex graphs.
fs: REST = ubiquitous default, gRPC = fast internal binary streaming, GraphQL = client picks fields.
fj: gRPC and GraphQL are just newer REST — both are strictly better for every use case.

followups.q: Why is gRPC awkward to call directly from a browser?
followups.a: Browsers can't control HTTP/2 framing (trailers, header manipulation) that gRPC depends on, so native gRPC doesn't work from `fetch`/`XMLHttpRequest`. You need `gRPC-Web`, which uses a different wire format and a proxy (Envoy) to translate to real gRPC. That extra hop and limited streaming support is why public-facing web APIs usually stay REST or GraphQL.

followups.q: How does GraphQL's typed schema help versioning compared to REST?
followups.a: GraphQL favors continuous evolution over versioned URLs: you add fields/types freely (additive = safe) and mark obsolete ones with `@deprecated`, since clients only request fields they name. Removing a field only breaks clients actually selecting it, which you can measure from query telemetry. REST typically bumps `/v2` for breaking changes because clients receive whole representations.

followups.q: When would you NOT pick GraphQL despite its flexibility?
followups.a: Skip GraphQL when responses are simple/uniform, when HTTP caching matters (everything is a `POST` to one URL, defeating CDN/`GET` caching), or when the team can't enforce query-cost/depth limits. File uploads, fine-grained per-endpoint rate limiting, and simple CRUD are all cleaner in REST. The flexibility isn't free — it shifts complexity to the server resolver layer.

q: Why is gRPC often faster than REST/JSON?
a: gRPC serializes messages as Protobuf, a compact binary format that is smaller and faster to encode/decode than text JSON. It runs over HTTP/2, which adds multiplexing (many calls on one connection), header compression, and binary framing. It also supports streaming (client, server, and bidirectional) natively. Benchmarks often show it several times faster than REST/JSON for high-volume internal traffic. The tradeoff: binary payloads are not human-readable, browser support needs gRPC-Web, and tooling is heavier.
fs: Protobuf binary + HTTP/2 multiplexing/streaming beats text JSON over HTTP/1.1.
fj: gRPC is faster because Google built it and it's newer — the underlying protocol doesn't really matter.

followups.q: How does HTTP/2 multiplexing avoid the head-of-line blocking that HTTP/1.1 has?
followups.a: HTTP/1.1 serializes requests on a connection (or opens many connections), so one slow response blocks those behind it. HTTP/2 splits streams into interleaved frames over one TCP connection, so independent calls progress concurrently. Note HOL blocking still exists at the TCP layer on packet loss — HTTP/3 (QUIC) fixes that by moving to per-stream UDP transport.

followups.q: Why is Protobuf smaller on the wire than JSON for the same data?
followups.a: Protobuf drops field names entirely — fields are referenced by integer tags defined in the `.proto` — and uses varint encoding so small numbers take one byte. JSON repeats every key as UTF-8 text and encodes all numbers as decimal strings. The cost is you can't decode a Protobuf message without its schema, unlike self-describing JSON.

followups.q: When might gRPC NOT be faster in practice?
followups.a: For low-volume or one-shot calls, the HTTP/2 connection setup and Protobuf codegen overhead can erase the win. If payloads are tiny, JSON parsing isn't your bottleneck — network RTT dominates. And public APIs paying the `gRPC-Web` proxy tax may see no benefit. The gains show up under sustained high-throughput internal traffic, not occasional requests.

q: What does idempotency mean for an HTTP API, and which methods are idempotent?
a: An operation is idempotent if making the same request multiple times has the same effect on server state as making it once. GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, and OPTIONS are idempotent by spec; POST and PATCH are generally not. Note idempotent is not the same as 'safe': GET/HEAD are safe (no state change), while DELETE is idempotent but not safe. Idempotency matters because clients retry on timeouts and network failures, and idempotent operations can be retried safely without side effects like duplicate records.
fs: Same request repeated = same end state; PUT/DELETE/GET idempotent, POST/PATCH not.
fj: Idempotent means the response is always the same — same request, same reply every time.

followups.q: Why does idempotency constrain the response code but not require an identical response body?
followups.a: Idempotency is about server *state*, not the response. A second `DELETE` leaves the resource gone (same state) but may return `404` instead of `204` — different responses, still idempotent. Likewise repeating a `PUT` may return a fresh `ETag`. The contract is 'no additional side effect,' not 'byte-identical reply.'

followups.q: Can a PATCH ever be idempotent? Give an example of each.
followups.a: Yes if you design it that way. `PATCH {status: 'shipped'}` is idempotent — setting the same value twice yields the same state. But `PATCH` with a JSON Patch op like `{op:'add', path:'/tags/-', value:'x'}` appends each time, so it's not. Idempotency depends on the operation's semantics, not just the verb.

followups.q: Why is DELETE idempotent but not safe?
followups.a: 'Safe' means no observable state change at all (read-only); `DELETE` clearly mutates state, so it isn't safe. 'Idempotent' means repeating it lands on the same end state — the resource is gone after one call or ten. The two properties are orthogonal: `GET` is both, `DELETE` is only idempotent, `POST` is typically neither.

q: How do you make a POST (e.g. a payment) safe to retry?
a: Use an idempotency key. The client generates a unique key (typically a UUIDv4) and sends it in an Idempotency-Key header. On first receipt, the server processes the request, stores the result keyed by that value, and returns it. If the same key arrives again (a retry), the server returns the stored result instead of creating a duplicate charge. Keys should expire after a window (e.g. 24h), and you must lock/dedupe concurrently arriving duplicates. Stripe and many payment APIs use exactly this pattern.
fs: Client sends Idempotency-Key; server stores result per key and replays it on retries.
fj: Just check if a charge already exists before running it — no need for a special idempotency key.

followups.q: What should the server do if the same key arrives with a DIFFERENT request body?
followups.a: Reject it — usually `422` or `409` — because reusing a key for a new payload signals a client bug or attack. Stripe hashes the original request and compares; a mismatch returns an error rather than silently replaying the old result or processing the new one. The key must bind to one specific operation.

followups.q: Why is the lock in the code necessary, and what breaks without it?
followups.a: Without the lock, two concurrent retries can both miss the cache (`store.get` returns nothing) and both call `charge`, producing a double charge — the exact bug the key was meant to prevent. The lock serializes duplicates so only the first proceeds; the second waits or returns `busy` to retry. The store check alone has a read-then-act race.

followups.q: Why give idempotency keys an expiry instead of keeping them forever?
followups.a: Keys are only needed during the retry window (seconds to hours), so storing them indefinitely bloats the store unboundedly. A 24h TTL covers realistic retries while reclaiming space. The tradeoff: a retry arriving after expiry could re-execute, so the window must comfortably exceed your client's max retry horizon.

q: Explain OAuth2 vs a plain JWT. How do they relate?
a: OAuth2 is an authorization framework: it defines flows (e.g. Authorization Code with PKCE, Client Credentials) by which a client obtains an access token to act on a resource owner's behalf, without seeing their password. JWT is just a token format: a signed, base64url-encoded JSON object with header, claims, and signature. OAuth2 often issues JWTs as access tokens, but it can use opaque tokens too. So they answer different questions: OAuth2 = how you get and delegate a token; JWT = how a token is encoded and verified. JWTs are self-contained, so the resource server validates the signature locally without a DB lookup.
fs: OAuth2 = delegation flows; JWT = a token format OAuth2 may issue. Different layers.
fj: OAuth and JWT are basically the same thing — JWT is just the token format OAuth uses.

followups.q: What's the trade-off between OAuth2 issuing a JWT vs an opaque access token?
followups.a: A JWT is self-contained, so resource servers validate it locally (fast, no network call) but can't easily revoke it before expiry. An opaque token carries no data — the resource server calls the auth server's introspection endpoint to validate, enabling instant revocation at the cost of a lookup per request. Choose JWT for scale/latency, opaque for tight revocation control.

followups.q: Where does OpenID Connect fit relative to OAuth2 and JWT?
followups.a: OIDC is an authentication layer on top of OAuth2's authorization framework. It adds an `id_token` — a JWT with standardized identity claims (`sub`, `email`, `iss`, `aud`) — proving *who* the user is, whereas OAuth2's access token only grants *what* the client may do. So OIDC reuses JWT-as-format and OAuth2-as-flow to answer 'who logged in.'

followups.q: Why is it a mistake to use an OAuth2 access token as a login/identity assertion?
followups.a: Access tokens are meant for the resource server, not the client, and their format/audience aren't guaranteed to identify the user (they may be opaque). Treating one as proof of login invites the 'confused deputy' problem where a token minted for app A is replayed to log into app B. Use the OIDC `id_token`, validated against your `aud`, for identity.

q: What are the main parts of a JWT and how is it validated?
a: A JWT has three dot-separated parts: header (alg/type), payload (claims like sub, exp, iat, iss, aud), and signature. To validate, the server recomputes the signature over header.payload using the secret (HS256) or public key (RS256) and checks it matches; then it verifies claims: exp not expired, iss and aud match expected values, and nbf if present. The payload is only base64url-encoded, not encrypted, so never put secrets in it. Use short-lived access tokens plus a refresh token, and prevent 'alg: none' downgrade attacks by pinning the expected algorithm.
fs: Verify signature with the right key, then check exp/iss/aud; payload is signed, not secret.
fj: The JWT payload is encrypted, so sensitive data is safe inside it — and I use whatever algorithm the token header says.

followups.q: Explain the RS256 vs HS256 'alg confusion' attack and how pinning stops it.
followups.a: If a server reads `alg` from the token and your config has the RS256 public key, an attacker can craft a token signed with HS256 using that public key as the HMAC secret — the server then verifies it 'successfully.' Pinning `algorithms: ['RS256']` forces asymmetric verification, so an HS256 forgery is rejected before the key is misused. Never let the token choose its own algorithm.

followups.q: Why prefer RS256 over HS256 for tokens consumed by many services?
followups.a: HS256 is symmetric: every service that *verifies* also holds the *signing* secret, so any one of them can mint tokens and a single leak compromises all. RS256 splits keys — the auth server holds the private signing key, while resource servers hold only the public verification key. That least-privilege split is why distributed systems favor RS256 (or ES256).

followups.q: Why is checking the `aud` claim critical even with a valid signature?
followups.a: A correctly signed token issued for a different service (`aud: api://billing`) is still cryptographically valid — without an `aud` check, your orders API would accept it, letting a billing token act on orders. Verifying `aud` matches *your* identifier scopes the token to its intended recipient and blocks token-passing/confused-deputy attacks across services.

q: Why use short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens?
a: Access tokens are usually self-contained JWTs validated locally, so they cannot be cheaply revoked before they expire. Keeping them short-lived (minutes) limits the blast radius if one leaks. A longer-lived refresh token, stored securely and exchanged at the auth server for new access tokens, lets sessions persist without re-login. The auth server can revoke refresh tokens and rotate them on each use (refresh token rotation) to detect theft. This separates 'frequent, cheap, local checks' from 'rare, revocable, server-side checks'.
fs: Short access tokens cap leak damage; revocable rotating refresh tokens keep sessions alive.
fj: I'd issue one long-lived access token — no need to rotate or revoke unless there's a breach.

followups.q: How does refresh token rotation detect a stolen refresh token?
followups.a: Each refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one. If both the legitimate client and a thief use the same token, the second redemption presents an already-consumed token — the auth server detects reuse and revokes the entire token family, forcing re-login. Without rotation, a stolen long-lived refresh token grants indefinite access silently.

followups.q: Where should a refresh token be stored in a browser SPA, and why?
followups.a: Prefer an `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite` cookie so JavaScript (and thus XSS) can't read it; storing it in `localStorage` exposes it to any injected script. Better still, use a backend-for-frontend that holds the refresh token server-side and the browser only ever sees a session cookie. The threat model is XSS exfiltration, which `HttpOnly` directly mitigates.

followups.q: What's the downside of making access tokens TOO short-lived?
followups.a: Sub-minute lifetimes mean constant refresh round-trips, adding latency and load on the auth server, and a refresh failing mid-session can interrupt the user. You also amplify the impact of auth-server outages. The sweet spot (often 5–15 min) balances revocation responsiveness against refresh chattiness — short enough to cap leak damage, long enough to avoid thrashing.

q: What are common strategies for API versioning?
a: URI path versioning (/v1/orders) is the most visible and easy to route/cache, but couples version to the URL. Header/media-type versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json) keeps URLs clean and is more RESTful but is harder to test in a browser. Query-param versioning (?version=2) is simple but easy to omit. Many teams default to URL path versioning for clarity and only bump the major version on breaking changes, layering additive (non-breaking) changes within a version. Whatever you choose, document the deprecation policy and sunset dates.
fs: Path versioning is the pragmatic default; bump major only on breaking changes.
fj: I'd create a new /v2 whenever I add or change anything — better to version than risk breaking clients.

followups.q: Why do REST purists prefer media-type versioning over URL path versioning?
followups.a: In REST a URI should identify a *resource*, not a representation; `/v1/orders` and `/v2/orders` point at the same order yet read as different resources, breaking the uniform-interface ideal. Media-type versioning (`Accept: vnd.api.v2+json`) keeps one canonical URL and negotiates the representation via content negotiation. The cost is it's invisible and hard to test in a browser.

followups.q: How can you avoid versioning the whole API when only one endpoint changes?
followups.a: Version at the resource or field level instead of globally: evolve additively within the version (new optional fields), and where a real break is needed, introduce a new field/endpoint alongside the old (`/orders` keeps working, `/orders` gains `total_v2`). Per-resource media types or a per-endpoint version header also localize the bump, so untouched endpoints keep their stable contract.

followups.q: What's the operational cost of running multiple major versions at once?
followups.a: Every live version multiplies your test matrix, security-patch surface, and on-call burden, since each must keep working and be monitored. Shared code paths accumulate version branches that rot. That's why teams cap the number of supported versions, publish sunset dates, and aggressively migrate consumers — the goal is to retire old versions, not let them pile up indefinitely.

q: What changes are backward-compatible vs breaking in an API?
a: Backward-compatible (safe, additive): adding a new optional field to a response, adding a new optional request parameter with a default, adding a new endpoint, or adding a new enum value clients are told to tolerate. Breaking: removing or renaming a field, changing a field's type or meaning, making an optional parameter required, tightening validation, changing default behavior, or altering status codes. The rule of thumb (Postel/robustness + 'tolerant reader'): consumers must ignore unknown fields, and producers must not remove or repurpose existing ones within a version.
fs: Additive optional changes are safe; removals/renames/type or semantic changes are breaking.
fj: Renaming a field is non-breaking — clients just update their code to use the new name.

followups.q: Why can adding a new enum value actually break some clients?
followups.a: Strict clients that validate responses against a closed enum (e.g. a generated type or a `switch` with no default) will reject or crash on an unseen value like `status: 'partially_refunded'`. It's only safe if you've contracted consumers to treat enums as open and tolerate unknowns. That's why some teams document 'expect new enum values' as part of the compatibility policy.

followups.q: Why is tightening validation a breaking change even though it 'improves' correctness?
followups.a: Clients that previously sent payloads your server accepted (a 21-char field, a missing optional you now require) suddenly get `4xx` — their working integration breaks even though they changed nothing. Stricter rules retroactively invalidate in-flight traffic. Roll tightened validation out as warnings first, or apply it only behind a new version, never silently in place.

followups.q: How does the 'tolerant reader' principle let producers evolve safely?
followups.a: If consumers ignore unknown fields and don't assume field order or exhaustiveness, producers can add fields freely without coordination. The robustness principle ('be liberal in what you accept') decouples the two sides so additive changes never need a lockstep deploy. Brittle clients that hard-fail on extra fields negate this, forcing versioning for changes that should've been free.

q: How would you deprecate an API field or endpoint gracefully?
a: Announce early and keep the old behavior working during a sunset window. Mark the field as deprecated in docs and the OpenAPI spec, and signal it at runtime via the Deprecation and Sunset HTTP headers (and a Warning). Keep emitting both old and new fields for a transition period so consumers migrate incrementally. Monitor usage by client/version to know who still calls the deprecated path, reach out to them, and only remove it after traffic drops to near zero and the sunset date passes. Never remove silently.
fs: Deprecate with Deprecation/Sunset headers + docs, dual-write, monitor usage, then remove.
fj: Once the announced sunset date passes, I remove the field — that's what we committed to publicly.

followups.q: What's the difference between the Deprecation and Sunset HTTP headers?
followups.a: `Deprecation` signals that the resource/field *is* deprecated (optionally with a date it became so), telling clients to stop relying on it. `Sunset` (RFC 8594) gives the future date/time after which it will *stop working*. Deprecation says 'don't use this anymore'; Sunset says 'it disappears on this date' — together they give consumers both the warning and the deadline.

followups.q: Why monitor per-client usage before removing, instead of just honoring the sunset date?
followups.a: The sunset date is a target, not a guarantee everyone migrated. Per-client telemetry tells you *who* still calls the deprecated path so you can contact them and avoid breaking a critical customer who missed the notice. Removing blind on the date can take down a paying integration; data-driven removal lets you slip the date or force-migrate stragglers safely.

followups.q: How do you handle a deprecated field that can't be dual-emitted because its meaning changed?
followups.a: If old and new semantics conflict (e.g. `amount` switching from dollars to cents), you can't dual-write the same key. Introduce a *new* field (`amount_cents`) alongside the unchanged old one, migrate consumers to it, then sunset the original. Never repurpose an existing field's meaning in place — silent semantic drift breaks clients with no error to alert them.

q: Compare token bucket and sliding-window rate limiting.
a: Token bucket: a bucket holds up to N tokens, refilled at a steady rate; each request consumes a token, and an empty bucket means reject (429). It allows short bursts up to the bucket size while bounding the average rate, and is used by Stripe and AWS. Fixed window counts requests per calendar window but allows a 2x spike at the boundary. Sliding window log/counter tracks request timestamps within the last N seconds for smoother enforcement, at higher memory cost. Token bucket is the common default; sliding window when you must avoid boundary bursts.
fs: Token bucket allows bounded bursts cheaply; sliding window is smoother but costlier.
fj: A fixed window counter is fine for rate limiting — count requests per minute and reset at the boundary.

followups.q: Exactly how does the fixed-window 2x burst at the boundary happen?
followups.a: A 100/min limit allows 100 requests at 0:59 and another 100 at 1:00 — 200 requests in a ~2-second span straddling the reset. Each window's counter is satisfied independently, so the limit is respected per-window but the *rate* across the boundary doubles. Sliding-window counters fix this by weighting the previous window's count into the current one.

followups.q: How do you implement token bucket without storing a refill timer per request?
followups.a: Use lazy refill: store only `tokens` and `last_refill_ts`. On each request compute `tokens = min(capacity, tokens + (now - last_refill) * rate)`, decrement if ≥1, and update the timestamp — all in one atomic step (e.g. a Redis Lua script). No background timer or per-tick job is needed; the bucket 'catches up' on access.

followups.q: Why is the sliding-window *log* more memory-hungry than the *counter* variant?
followups.a: The log stores every request timestamp in the window so it can count exactly how many fall in the trailing N seconds — O(requests) memory per client. The counter approximation keeps just two integers (current and previous window counts) and interpolates, trading a little accuracy at the boundary for O(1) memory. At high QPS per key, the log's cost is prohibitive.

q: What status code and headers signal rate limiting to a client?
a: Return 429 Too Many Requests when a client exceeds its limit. Include a Retry-After header (seconds or an HTTP date) telling the client when to retry. Most APIs also expose informational headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (cap per window), X-RateLimit-Remaining (calls left), and X-RateLimit-Reset (when the window resets). Well-behaved clients read Retry-After and back off rather than hammering. For server overload (not per-client limits) 503 with Retry-After is more appropriate.
fs: 429 + Retry-After, plus X-RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset for client self-throttling.
fj: I'd return 403 or 500 for throttling — no need for a Retry-After header.

followups.q: When should you use 503 instead of 429 for throttling?
followups.a: Use `429` when a *specific client* exceeds its per-client quota — the fault is that caller's rate. Use `503 Service Unavailable` when the *server as a whole* is overloaded or in maintenance and is shedding load regardless of who's calling. Both pair with `Retry-After`, but `429` says 'you're sending too much,' `503` says 'we can't serve anyone right now.'

followups.q: Why is returning 403 or 500 for throttling actively harmful to clients?
followups.a: `403` tells a client it's *forbidden*, so it stops retrying permanently instead of backing off and resuming — losing legitimate traffic. `500` looks like a server bug, triggering aggressive retries that worsen the overload. Neither carries `Retry-After`, so clients can't pace themselves. `429` is the only code that signals 'transient, retry later' with timing.

followups.q: Should Retry-After use seconds or an HTTP date, and what's the gotcha?
followups.a: Both are valid; seconds (`Retry-After: 30`) is simpler and avoids clock-skew issues, while an HTTP date requires the client's clock to roughly match the server's. The gotcha with seconds is many clients ignore it and use their own backoff, so treat it as advisory and still enforce limits server-side. For absolute reset times, `X-RateLimit-Reset` complements it.

q: Compare offset pagination with cursor (keyset) pagination.
a: Offset pagination (LIMIT N OFFSET M) is simple and allows jumping to any page, but the database must scan and discard M rows, so deep pages get slow (O(offset)), and inserts/deletes between requests cause skipped or duplicated rows. Cursor/keyset pagination remembers the last seen sort key (e.g. created_at,id) and queries WHERE (created_at,id) > (last) ORDER BY ... LIMIT N. It seeks directly via an index, giving stable, near-constant performance regardless of depth, but you can't jump to an arbitrary page. Use cursor pagination for large, frequently changing, or deeply paginated datasets.
fs: Offset scans+discards and drifts; keyset seeks by indexed sort key for stable deep paging.
fj: OFFSET is simpler, so I use it everywhere — if page 5000 is slow we can optimize later.

code.label: Keyset page query
lines: ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC

followups.q: Why does the keyset query use a composite (created_at, id) instead of just created_at?
followups.a: `created_at` alone isn't unique — multiple rows can share a timestamp, so paging on it risks skipping or repeating rows at the boundary. Adding the unique `id` as a tie-breaker makes the sort key total and deterministic, and the row-value comparison `(created_at, id) < (...)` cleanly resumes after the exact last row. The index should cover both columns in the same order.

followups.q: Why exactly does deep offset pagination cause skipped or duplicated rows under writes?
followups.a: Offset counts *positions*, not identities. If a row is inserted before your current offset between page fetches, every subsequent row shifts down by one — you re-see a row you already got (duplicate). A deletion shifts them up, skipping one. Keyset anchors on the last row's *value*, so concurrent inserts/deletes elsewhere don't shift your resume point.

followups.q: What feature do you lose with keyset pagination, and how do you work around needing it?
followups.a: You can't jump to an arbitrary page number (e.g. 'page 500') because there's no offset — only next/prev relative to a cursor. If a UI truly needs page jumps, approximate with indexed range queries or precomputed counts, or accept that deep random-access pages are rarely used and offer search/filter instead. Most feeds only need 'next,' which keyset handles perfectly.

q: How should a paginated API return cursors to clients?
a: Return an opaque cursor, typically a base64url-encoded payload holding the last sort key(s) and direction, plus a next link or next_cursor. Treat it as opaque so clients don't parse or fabricate it and you can change its internals later. Optionally HMAC-sign the cursor with a server secret so tampering is detectable and rejected. Include a tie-breaker (a unique column like id) in the sort to keep ordering deterministic. Don't leak raw internal IDs or row offsets, and document whether the cursor is stable across data changes.
fs: Opaque, optionally HMAC-signed cursor encoding (sort_key,id); return next_cursor not page numbers.
fj: I'd just use the raw DB offset as the cursor — clients can parse it if they want.

followups.q: Why make the cursor opaque instead of a readable JSON object the client can build?
followups.a: Opacity is an API contract: if clients parse or construct cursors, your internal sort keys become a public interface you can never change without breaking them. Base64-encoding (ideally signed) signals 'don't touch this,' so you're free to switch from `created_at,id` to a different key or add fields later. Readable cursors invite clients to forge out-of-range or injection values.

followups.q: What does HMAC-signing the cursor protect against that opacity alone doesn't?
followups.a: Opacity is just obscurity — a curious client can base64-decode and tamper. An HMAC over the payload lets the server *detect* modification and reject forged cursors, preventing clients from crafting one that skips access controls or probes other users' data. It's the difference between 'please don't edit this' and 'editing it fails verification.'

followups.q: Should a cursor's results stay stable if data changes mid-pagination, and how do you communicate that?
followups.a: It depends on the cursor type: a keyset cursor on `created_at,id` gives a stable 'as-of' walk where new inserts may appear or not depending on position, while a snapshot cursor freezes the result set. Neither is universally right, so *document* the guarantee — clients building infinite scroll need to know whether they'll see duplicates or miss rows under concurrent writes.

q: When do you use 2xx vs 4xx vs 5xx status codes?
a: 2xx = success: 200 OK, 201 Created (resource made, often with Location), 202 Accepted (async, not done yet), 204 No Content. 4xx = client error, don't blindly retry as-is: 400 bad request, 401 unauthenticated, 403 forbidden, 404 not found, 409 conflict, 422 unprocessable (validation), 429 too many requests. 5xx = server error, often retryable: 500 internal, 502 bad gateway, 503 unavailable (with Retry-After), 504 gateway timeout. The dividing line: 4xx means the client must change something; 5xx means the server failed and the same request might succeed later.
fs: 4xx = caller's fault (fix request); 5xx = server's fault (often retryable).
fj: I return 200 with an 'error' field in the body — status codes are just for HTTP routing.

followups.q: Why is returning 200 with an error body in the response harmful?
followups.a: Generic clients, proxies, monitoring, and retry middleware all key off the status code — a `200` tells them 'success,' so failures go uncounted in error dashboards, caches may store the bad response, and retries never fire. It forces every consumer to parse the body to learn the truth. The status code *is* the machine-readable outcome; don't override it with a lie.

followups.q: When is 400 vs 422 the right choice for invalid input?
followups.a: `400 Bad Request` fits syntactically malformed requests — unparseable JSON, missing required structure. `422 Unprocessable Entity` fits well-formed requests that fail *semantic* validation — valid JSON but `age: -5` or a referenced ID that doesn't exist. It lets clients tell 'I sent garbage' from 'my data was understood but rejected.' Many APIs use `400` for both, but `422` is more precise.

followups.q: Which 5xx codes are safe to retry, and which aren't necessarily?
followups.a: `502`, `503`, and `504` are classic transient/upstream failures where the same request may succeed shortly, so retry with backoff (`503` even gives `Retry-After`). `500` is ambiguous — it might be a deterministic bug that fails identically every time, or it may have partially applied a non-idempotent write, so retry it only if the operation is idempotent or idempotency-keyed.

q: What's the difference between 401 and 403?
a: 401 Unauthorized means 'not authenticated' — the request lacks valid credentials or the token is missing/expired/invalid; the response should include a WWW-Authenticate header, and the fix is to log in or refresh the token. 403 Forbidden means 'authenticated but not allowed' — the server knows who you are but you lack permission for this resource, and retrying with the same credentials won't help. The naming is historically confusing (401 literally says 'Unauthorized' but means unauthenticated), so interviewers love this one.
fs: 401 = who are you (authenticate); 403 = I know you but you can't (authorize).
fj: I return 401 when someone lacks permission and 403 when their token is missing or invalid.

followups.q: Why does a 401 require a WWW-Authenticate header but a 403 doesn't?
followups.a: `401` invites the client to *authenticate*, so the spec requires `WWW-Authenticate` to advertise the accepted scheme (`Bearer`, `Basic`, realm, error detail). A `403` is final — the client is already authenticated and simply lacks permission, so there's no challenge to issue and re-authenticating wouldn't help. The header is the actionable 'here's how to get in.'

followups.q: When might you deliberately return 404 instead of 403 for a forbidden resource?
followups.a: To avoid leaking existence: a `403` confirms the resource exists, which lets an attacker enumerate valid IDs they can't access. Returning `404` hides whether the resource is real, common for private objects or multi-tenant isolation. The trade-off is debuggability — legitimate users get a confusing 'not found' for something that exists, so it's a security-vs-clarity choice.

followups.q: Should an expired token return 401 or 403, and why does it matter?
followups.a: `401` — an expired token means you're no longer authenticated, and the fix is to refresh/re-login, which `401` + `WWW-Authenticate` signals. Returning `403` would tell the client 'you're authenticated but forbidden,' so it would never attempt a refresh and the session would dead-end. Getting this right is what makes silent token refresh work.

q: When is 201 vs 202 vs 204 the right success code?
a: 201 Created: a request synchronously created a new resource; return it (or a reference) and a Location header pointing to it. 202 Accepted: the request was accepted for asynchronous processing and is not complete yet — common for jobs/queues; you typically return a status URL the client can poll. 204 No Content: success with intentionally no body, common for DELETE or a PUT that updates without returning the entity. Choosing precisely tells clients whether to read a body, poll for completion, or follow a Location.
fs: 201 done+created (Location), 202 queued (poll later), 204 done with no body.
fj: I just return 200 for every successful write — it's the standard success code.

followups.q: For a 202, what should the response give the client and how do they learn the outcome?
followups.a: Return a status/job URL (often in `Location` or a body field) the client polls; that endpoint reports `pending`/`running`/`done`/`failed` and, on completion, links to or returns the created resource. The `202` itself only says 'accepted,' not 'succeeded' — the eventual result, including failures that happen during async processing, surfaces through the status resource, not the original response.

followups.q: Why does 204 explicitly forbid a body, and when is 200 better for the same write?
followups.a: `204 No Content` is a contract that there's intentionally nothing to read, so compliant clients won't even attempt to parse — sending bytes violates it. Use `200` instead when the write *does* return something useful, like the updated entity with a new `ETag` or server-computed fields. Choose `204` only when the client gains nothing from a body (a fire-and-forget `DELETE`).

followups.q: Why include a Location header on a 201, and what should it point to?
followups.a: `Location` tells the client the canonical URL of the just-created resource (`/orders/abc`), so it can fetch, update, or link to it without guessing the ID scheme. It turns 'created' into 'created *here*.' Returning the resource body too is fine, but the header is the machine-readable handle — omitting it forces clients to parse the body or re-query to find what they made.

q: How should clients implement retries with timeouts safely?
a: Set an explicit per-request timeout so a hung call fails fast instead of holding resources. Retry only idempotent or idempotency-keyed requests, and only on transient failures (timeouts, connection errors, 502/503/504, 429). Use exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering-herd synchronization, honor Retry-After when present, and cap the number of attempts and total deadline. Pair retries with a circuit breaker so you stop hammering a clearly-down dependency. Blindly retrying every error (including 400/409) or retrying non-idempotent POSTs causes duplicates and cascading overload.
fs: Timeout + bounded exponential backoff with jitter, only on transient/idempotent calls, honor Retry-After.
fj: If a request fails, just retry it right away — including POST requests, since the client needs a response.

followups.q: Why is a per-attempt timeout insufficient without an overall deadline budget?
followups.a: With only per-attempt timeouts, N retries each waiting the full timeout plus backoff can blow your total latency budget (3 retries × 2s + backoff = many seconds), and nested layers multiply it. An overall deadline caps the *aggregate* time so the call fails predictably and you stop retrying once the budget is spent, even if attempts remain. Propagate the remaining deadline down the call chain.

followups.q: Why is a timeout that fires NOT a safe signal that the server didn't process the request?
followups.a: A timeout means you gave up waiting — the server may have completed the work but the response was lost or slow. So retrying a non-idempotent `POST` after a timeout can double-apply it. That's exactly why timeouts should only auto-retry idempotent or idempotency-keyed operations; for others, treat the outcome as *unknown* and reconcile rather than blindly resend.

followups.q: Why should retries happen at one layer rather than at every layer of the stack?
followups.a: If the client, the gateway, and a service each retry 3×, a single transient failure becomes 27 amplified requests — a retry storm that hammers a struggling dependency. Concentrate retry logic at one well-chosen layer (often the edge client) and have inner layers fail fast, so total fan-out stays bounded. This is why budgets and a circuit breaker matter more than retry count alone.

q: Why add jitter to exponential backoff?
a: Pure exponential backoff makes all clients that failed at the same instant retry at the same future instants (1s, 2s, 4s...), so they synchronize and slam the recovering server in waves — a thundering herd that prevents recovery. Adding randomness (jitter) spreads retries across the interval so load is smooth. 'Full jitter' picks a random delay in [0, base] each attempt. AWS's analysis showed full/equal jitter dramatically reduces contention and completes work faster than backoff alone. It's a one-line change with outsized resilience benefits.
fs: Jitter de-synchronizes retries so recovering servers aren't hit by simultaneous waves.
fj: Exponential backoff is enough on its own — if every client doubles its wait time, they'll naturally spread out.

followups.q: What's the difference between 'full jitter' and 'equal jitter'?
followups.a: Full jitter sleeps a uniform random delay in `[0, base]`, maximizing spread but occasionally retrying almost immediately. Equal jitter sleeps `base/2 + random(0, base/2)`, guaranteeing at least half the backoff so it never retries too eagerly while still de-synchronizing. AWS found full jitter completes work fastest with least contention; equal jitter trades spread for a minimum wait.

followups.q: Why doesn't jitter help if all clients still cap at the same max backoff?
followups.a: Once attempts hit the ceiling (say `cap=30s`), without jitter every client retries every ~30s in lockstep again, re-synchronizing into waves even after early spreading. The fix is to keep applying jitter *to the capped value* too (`random(0, cap)`), so even saturated retries stay scattered. Jitter must apply at the ceiling, not just on the exponential ramp.

followups.q: Can jitter ever hurt — e.g. make a deadline-sensitive call worse?
followups.a: Yes: full jitter can occasionally pick a near-zero delay (too eager, re-amplifying load) or a near-max delay that blows a tight latency budget. For deadline-sensitive paths, equal jitter (guaranteed minimum wait) or clamping the random range gives more predictable timing. Jitter optimizes *aggregate* server health, sometimes at the cost of any single call's worst-case latency.

q: How do webhooks work and how do you secure them?
a: A webhook is a server-to-server push: when an event occurs, the provider POSTs a payload to a URL you registered, so you don't have to poll. Secure them by verifying authenticity: the provider signs the raw request body with a shared secret using HMAC-SHA256 and sends the signature (and a timestamp) in a header; your endpoint recomputes the HMAC over the exact raw bytes and compares using a constant-time check. Also reject old timestamps to stop replay, return 2xx fast and process async, and make handling idempotent because providers retry and may deliver duplicates or out-of-order events.
fs: Verify HMAC over raw body + timestamp, dedupe by event id, ack fast and process async.
fj: I process the webhook payload as it arrives — the provider is already authenticated so signature verification is redundant.

followups.q: Why must you HMAC the RAW bytes, not the parsed-then-reserialized JSON?
followups.a: Parsing and re-serializing can reorder keys, change whitespace, or normalize numbers, producing different bytes than the provider signed — so the HMAC won't match. You must capture the exact raw request body *before* any JSON middleware touches it. This is a classic webhook bug: signature verification fails intermittently because a body parser ran first.

followups.q: Why use hmac.compare_digest instead of a plain == comparison?
followups.a: `==` on strings short-circuits at the first differing byte, so the time it takes leaks how many leading bytes matched — a timing side channel an attacker can exploit to forge a signature byte by byte. `compare_digest` runs in constant time regardless of where the mismatch is, closing that channel. Security-sensitive equality checks should always be constant-time.

followups.q: How does including a timestamp in the signature prevent replay attacks?
followups.a: Sign `timestamp + body` together and reject requests whose timestamp is older than a tolerance (e.g. 5 min). Without it, an attacker who captures one valid signed request could resend it forever. Binding the timestamp into the HMAC means it can't be altered without breaking the signature, and the freshness window bounds how long a captured request stays replayable.

q: Why must webhook consumers be idempotent?
a: Webhook providers guarantee at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once: if your endpoint is slow, times out, or returns a non-2xx, they retry, so the same event can arrive multiple times. Networks can also reorder events. If your handler isn't idempotent, retries create duplicate records (double-charged orders, double emails). The fix: each event has a unique id; record processed ids and skip duplicates, or design the side effect to be naturally idempotent (upsert by event id). Acknowledge with 2xx only after you've durably accepted the event.
fs: Delivery is at-least-once with retries, so dedupe by event id or use upserts.
fj: Each webhook fires exactly once, so I just process it when it arrives — no deduplication needed.

followups.q: Why ack with 2xx only AFTER durably accepting, not after fully processing?
followups.a: If you process synchronously before acking, slow work can exceed the provider's timeout — it retries while you're still working, causing duplicates and tying up the connection. Instead, durably *persist* the event (to a queue/DB), ack `2xx` immediately, then process async. You ack 'I won't lose this,' not 'I'm done.' Acking before persisting risks losing the event on a crash.

followups.q: How do you handle events that arrive OUT OF ORDER, not just duplicated?
followups.a: Idempotency alone doesn't fix ordering — a `payment.refunded` before `payment.created` corrupts state. Carry a version/sequence or timestamp per resource and ignore events older than the state you've applied (last-write-wins by version), or reconcile against the provider's current state on conflict. Design handlers to converge to the latest truth regardless of arrival order.

followups.q: What's the trade-off between a dedup table of processed IDs and a natural upsert?
followups.a: A processed-ID table works for any side effect but needs its own write, TTL/cleanup, and a check-then-act that must be transactional with the work. A natural upsert (`INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO NOTHING`) folds dedup into the same atomic write — simpler and race-free — but only works when the side effect is expressible as an idempotent DB write, not for things like sending an email.

q: How should an API validate incoming requests?
a: Validate at the boundary against a schema before any business logic: types, required fields, formats, ranges, enum values, and string lengths. Use a declarative schema (JSON Schema / OpenAPI, or a library like zod/pydantic) so validation is consistent and self-documenting. Reject invalid input with 400 (malformed) or 422 (well-formed but semantically invalid) and return a structured error listing which fields failed. Allowlist expected fields rather than trusting input, and never rely on client-side validation. This is also a security control: it blocks injection and malformed payloads early.
fs: Schema-validate at the edge, allowlist fields, return 400/422 with per-field errors.
fj: The front end already validates the input, so on the server I just process whatever body comes in.

followups.q: Why allowlist fields instead of just validating the ones you expect?
followups.a: If you validate only known fields but pass the whole object through, extra attacker-supplied fields ride along into a DB or object assignment — the mass-assignment / over-posting bug (e.g. injecting `isAdmin: true`). Allowlisting strips anything not in the schema, so unexpected fields are dropped rather than trusted. Zod's `.strict()` rejects unknown keys outright for this reason.

followups.q: Why isn't schema validation alone enough — what does business validation add?
followups.a: Schema validation checks *shape* (types, ranges, formats) but can't know an email is already registered, a referenced order belongs to this user, or an amount exceeds a balance — those need DB/context. So you layer: cheap schema rejection at the edge (`400`/`422`), then business checks (`409`/`422`) deeper in. Schema validity alone lets logically-invalid-but-well-formed requests through.

followups.q: Why should validation errors return per-field detail, and what's the security caveat?
followups.a: Per-field errors (`{field:'age', error:'must be ≤120'}`) let clients fix input precisely instead of guessing, hugely improving DX. The caveat: don't leak internal details (stack traces, SQL, which records exist) in those messages, since validation responses are attacker-visible. Return structured, field-scoped, sanitized messages — helpful to legitimate callers, uninformative to probers.

q: What does an API gateway do?
a: An API gateway is a single entry point in front of backend services that handles cross-cutting concerns so each service doesn't reimplement them: routing/composition to the right service, authentication and authorization, rate limiting and throttling, TLS termination, request/response transformation, caching, and observability (logging, metrics, tracing). In microservices it decouples clients from internal topology and can aggregate calls. The tradeoff is it's a critical shared component and potential bottleneck/single point of failure, so it must be highly available and kept thin (avoid burying business logic in it).
fs: Single edge for routing, authn/z, rate limiting, TLS, observability — keep it thin and HA.
fj: An API gateway is basically a smart load balancer — you can put routing and business logic in it to centralize everything.

followups.q: How does an API gateway differ from a plain load balancer?
followups.a: A load balancer mostly works at L4/L7 to *distribute* traffic across identical backends by health and load. A gateway is application-aware: it routes by path/method to *different* services, enforces auth, rate limits, transforms payloads, aggregates calls, and emits per-API telemetry. A gateway often *sits behind* a load balancer; it orchestrates, not just spreads connections.

followups.q: Why is 'keep the gateway thin' such a strong rule?
followups.a: The gateway is shared by every service and on the critical path, so business logic baked into it becomes a deployment bottleneck, a single point of failure, and a place where team ownership blurs. Cross-cutting concerns (auth, limits, TLS) belong there; domain rules belong in services. A fat gateway turns into a distributed monolith where one change risks the whole edge.

followups.q: What's the danger of doing response aggregation (BFF-style) in the gateway?
followups.a: Aggregating multiple backend calls into one response couples the gateway to each service's schema and adds fan-out latency and partial-failure handling at the critical shared layer. If one backend is slow, the call stalls. Many teams push aggregation into a dedicated Backend-for-Frontend service instead, keeping the gateway thin and letting the BFF own composition and its failure modes.

q: Walk through what happens to a request as it passes through an API gateway.
a: A typical pipeline: (1) TLS termination and basic request parsing; (2) authentication — validate the JWT/API key and reject 401 if invalid; (3) authorization/scopes check, 403 if not allowed; (4) rate limiting / quota check, 429 if exceeded; (5) routing to the correct upstream service, possibly with request transformation or aggregation; (6) forward with a timeout and retry/circuit-breaker policy; (7) transform the response, attach correlation/trace IDs, and emit metrics and logs. Failing fast on auth and limits before touching backends protects the core services from bad or excessive traffic.
fs: TLS->authn->authz->rate limit->route w/ timeout->transform->observe; reject early to shield backends.
fj: I'd pass every request straight through to the backend and handle auth inside the service itself.

followups.q: Why does the order — auth before rate limiting — matter?
followups.a: Authenticating first lets you rate-limit *per identity* (per API key/user) rather than only per IP, which is far more accurate and abuse-resistant. It also rejects unauthenticated junk cheaply. The subtlety: auth itself can be expensive (signature/introspection), so you often put a cheap per-IP limit *before* auth to shield the auth check, then a per-identity limit after. Layering both is common.

followups.q: Where does the gateway add a correlation/trace ID, and why early?
followups.a: The gateway mints a correlation ID at ingress (or propagates an inbound one) and injects it into headers before forwarding, so every downstream log and span shares it for end-to-end tracing. Doing it early — before routing — means even requests rejected at auth/rate-limit stages are traceable. Without a gateway-assigned ID, you can't stitch a single request's journey across services.

followups.q: What happens to in-flight requests if the gateway itself needs to fail over?
followups.a: Because the gateway is the single edge, its failover must be seamless: run multiple gateway instances behind a load balancer/anycast so a dead instance just stops receiving new connections. In-flight requests on the dying instance are dropped and rely on client retries (idempotent ones recover cleanly). Stateless gateway design — no per-request state stuck on one node — is what makes this safe.

q: What are the core constraints of REST, and what does the Richardson Maturity Model add on top?
a: REST's architectural constraints are client-server separation, statelessness (each request carries its own context), cacheability, a uniform interface (resources, representations, self-descriptive messages), and a layered system; HATEOAS is the optional hypermedia constraint. The Richardson Maturity Model grades how 'RESTful' an API is: Level 0 is a single endpoint tunneling RPC, Level 1 introduces distinct resource URLs, Level 2 uses HTTP verbs and status codes correctly (where most 'REST' APIs sit), and Level 3 adds hypermedia controls (HATEOAS) so responses link to next actions. Most production APIs target Level 2 deliberately and skip full HATEOAS because few clients consume hypermedia links. The point is to use HTTP's semantics, not to chase a purity badge.
fs: Stateless + uniform interface + cacheable; Richardson L2 (verbs+status codes) is the pragmatic target, L3 adds HATEOAS.
fj: Any JSON over HTTP is REST — as long as you're using HTTP and returning JSON, you're doing REST.

followups.q: Why does statelessness enable horizontal scaling, and what's the cost?
followups.a: Because each request carries its own context (no session affinity), any instance can serve any request, so you scale by adding nodes behind a load balancer and a node death loses no session. The cost is larger requests (auth/context resent each time) and pushing state to the client or a shared store. You trade per-request overhead for trivial scaling and failover.

followups.q: Why do most teams deliberately stop at Level 2 and skip HATEOAS?
followups.a: Level 3 hypermedia requires clients to *discover* actions from links at runtime, but in practice clients are hardcoded against known URLs and ignore the links — so the cost buys little. HATEOAS shines for long-lived, loosely-coupled hypermedia clients (browsers), rare in API-to-API work. L2 (correct verbs + status codes + resource URLs) captures most of REST's real value.

followups.q: What concretely makes a 'REST' API actually Level 0 RPC in disguise?
followups.a: A single endpoint like `POST /api` whose body carries `{action: 'getUser', id: 5}`, returning `200` even on errors — it tunnels method calls over HTTP instead of using resources, verbs, and status codes. No distinct URLs (L1), no verb semantics (L2). It's SOAP-style RPC wearing JSON. The tell: the *verb and URL convey nothing*; all meaning lives in the body.

q: Walk through the semantics of GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. When is PUT vs PATCH correct?
a: GET reads a resource and is safe and idempotent. POST creates a subordinate resource or triggers a non-idempotent action and typically returns 201 with a Location. PUT replaces a resource entirely at a known URL and is idempotent, so the client must send the full representation. PATCH applies a partial modification (e.g. JSON Patch or JSON Merge Patch) and is generally not idempotent unless you design it to be. DELETE removes a resource and is idempotent. The PUT-vs-PATCH rule: PUT when the client owns the URL and sends the whole entity; PATCH when sending only the changed fields against a server-assigned resource.
fs: GET safe; PUT full-replace+idempotent; PATCH partial (often not idempotent); POST create/action; DELETE idempotent.
fj: POST works for all writes — and PATCH and PUT are basically the same thing, so I just pick one.

followups.q: What's the gotcha of using PUT for partial updates by sending only changed fields?
followups.a: `PUT` means *replace the whole resource*, so omitting fields should null/reset them — sending only `{name:'x'}` could wipe every other attribute. Clients that treat `PUT` like a partial update silently destroy data. If you only have the changed fields, use `PATCH`. `PUT` requires the *complete* representation precisely because it overwrites the entire entity.

followups.q: Compare JSON Merge Patch and JSON Patch — when does each fail?
followups.a: JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396) is simple — send the fields to change — but can't unambiguously remove a field (`null` conflicts with setting a real null) or modify array elements. JSON Patch (RFC 6902) is an explicit op list (`add`/`remove`/`replace`/`test`) handling those precisely, but verbose. Use Merge Patch for simple updates, JSON Patch for removals, array ops, or conditional `test`.

followups.q: Why is GET being 'safe' a contract servers must honor, and what breaks if violated?
followups.a: Safe means a `GET` causes no observable state change, so caches, crawlers, prefetchers, and link-followers feel free to issue it repeatedly without consequence. If a `GET` mutates state (e.g. `GET /delete?id=5`), a search engine bot or browser prefetch can silently trigger destructive actions. The safety contract is what lets infrastructure treat reads as freely repeatable.

q: What are the classic operational pitfalls of a GraphQL API and how do you mitigate them?
a: GraphQL's single flexible endpoint creates several traps. The N+1 problem: a nested query fans out into one DB call per parent, fixed with a per-request DataLoader that batches and caches. Unbounded query cost: a malicious or careless client can request deeply nested or huge result sets, so you enforce query depth limits, complexity/cost analysis, and pagination. Caching is harder than REST because everything is a POST to one URL, so you lean on persisted queries, response caching by query hash, and field-level caching. Errors are partial: a 200 can carry an 'errors' array alongside data, so clients must inspect it. Schema evolution favors deprecating fields over versioning.
fs: Watch N+1 (DataLoader), query-cost (depth/complexity limits), weak HTTP caching (persisted queries), partial errors.
fj: GraphQL is flexible so I wouldn't add depth or cost limits — and caching works the same way as REST.

followups.q: Why must the DataLoader's batch function return results in the same order as the input ids?
followups.a: DataLoader maps each requested key to a position in the returned array, so result `[i]` must correspond to `ids[i]`. A `whereIn` query returns rows in arbitrary DB order, so you must re-sort by mapping `ids` back over the rows (as the code does with `rows.find`). Returning them out of order silently associates the wrong data with the wrong key — a nasty correctness bug.

followups.q: Why is query depth limiting insufficient on its own, and what does complexity analysis add?
followups.a: A shallow query can still be catastrophically expensive — `users(first: 10000) { posts(first: 10000) }` is only 2 levels deep but requests 100M edges. Depth limits miss breadth. Complexity/cost analysis assigns a weight per field (multiplied by pagination args) and rejects queries over a budget, capturing the real work. You typically enforce *both* depth and cost limits together.

followups.q: Why is per-request scoping critical for DataLoader, and what bug appears if it's shared globally?
followups.a: DataLoader caches by key for its lifetime. A global instance shared across requests would serve one user stale/other-user data and never reflect mutations — a cache-poisoning and data-leak bug. You must create a fresh loader per request (in the GraphQL context) so its batching/caching window is exactly one operation. Reuse across requests defeats both correctness and authorization isolation.

q: Compare the OAuth2 Authorization Code (with PKCE) and Client Credentials flows. When do you use each?
a: Authorization Code with PKCE is for flows acting on behalf of a human: the user authenticates at the authorization server, which returns a short-lived code to the app's redirect URI, and the app exchanges that code (plus a PKCE verifier) for tokens. PKCE binds the code to the originating client so an intercepted code is useless, which is why it's now required for both public (mobile/SPA) and confidential clients. Client Credentials is for machine-to-machine where there is no user: the service authenticates with its own client_id/secret and gets an access token for its own identity. Use Authorization Code+PKCE for user-facing apps; Client Credentials for backend jobs and service-to-service calls. The deprecated Implicit and Resource Owner Password flows should not be used.
fs: Auth Code+PKCE = on behalf of a user (PKCE stops code interception); Client Credentials = machine-to-machine.
fj: For a browser app I'd use the Implicit flow — it's simpler. For a backend service I'd just use the user's password grant.

followups.q: Mechanically, how does PKCE stop an intercepted authorization code from being used?
followups.a: The client generates a random `code_verifier`, sends its hash (`code_challenge`) when requesting the code, then must present the original `verifier` when exchanging the code for tokens. An attacker who intercepts the code (e.g. via a malicious app on the redirect) lacks the verifier, so the token exchange fails. The challenge/verifier binds the code to the client that started the flow.

followups.q: Why was the Implicit flow deprecated in favor of Auth Code + PKCE for SPAs?
followups.a: Implicit returned the access token directly in the URL fragment — exposed to browser history, referrers, and any script — with no code exchange and no refresh token. PKCE lets even public SPAs use the more secure Authorization Code flow without a client secret, keeping the token out of the URL and enabling refresh-token rotation. That's why Implicit is now obsolete.

followups.q: Why is the Resource Owner Password Credentials grant considered an anti-pattern?
followups.a: It has the app collect the user's actual username/password — defeating OAuth's whole point of *not* exposing credentials to clients, and incompatible with MFA, federated login, or consent screens. It also trains users to type passwords into third-party UIs (phishing risk). Use Authorization Code + PKCE so the user authenticates directly with the identity provider.

q: When are API keys appropriate versus OAuth tokens, and how do you manage them safely?
a: API keys are a simple shared secret identifying a calling application or project, good for server-to-server access to your own API, partner integrations, and usage metering/rate-limiting per key. They authenticate the app, not an end user, and carry no standard scoping or expiry, so they're weaker than OAuth for delegated, user-scoped access. Manage them by issuing per-client (never one global key), supporting rotation and instant revocation, scoping each key to least privilege, transmitting only over TLS in a header (not the URL, which leaks into logs), and hashing keys at rest so a DB leak doesn't expose them. For acting on a user's behalf or fine-grained permissions, use OAuth tokens instead.
fs: API keys ID an app for S2S/metering; per-client, scoped, rotatable, hashed at rest, in a header not the URL.
fj: One API key shared across clients is simpler — put it in the query string so it's easy to pass and debug.

followups.q: Why hash API keys at rest when you don't hash, say, a JWT signing secret the same way?
followups.a: An API key is a *bearer credential you store per client* — if your DB leaks, plaintext keys let attackers impersonate every client. Hashing (the key is like a password) means a leak exposes only useless hashes; you verify by hashing the presented key. A JWT signing secret isn't stored per-client and must be usable to sign, so it's protected differently (KMS/vault), not one-way hashed.

followups.q: How do you rotate an API key without a hard cutover that breaks the client?
followups.a: Support multiple active keys per client: issue the new key, let the client deploy and start using it while the old one still works, then revoke the old key once traffic confirms migration. A grace period with overlapping valid keys turns rotation into a zero-downtime swap. A single-key model forces a synchronized cutover that risks an outage if the client deploys late.

followups.q: Why is putting an API key in the URL query string specifically dangerous?
followups.a: URLs are logged everywhere — access logs, proxies, browser history, `Referer` headers sent to third parties, and analytics — so the key leaks into many systems you don't control and can't easily scrub. A header (`Authorization`/`X-API-Key`) isn't logged by default and isn't forwarded via `Referer`. Same secret, but the transport channel determines how widely it leaks.

q: What is mutual TLS (mTLS) and where does it fit in API security?
a: Ordinary TLS authenticates only the server to the client; mutual TLS adds the reverse, so the client also presents an X.509 certificate that the server validates against a trusted CA. This gives strong, cryptographic identity for both parties at the transport layer, independent of any bearer token, which is why it's used for service-to-service auth inside a zero-trust mesh, partner B2B integrations, and high-assurance APIs (banking, payments). It also enables certificate-bound access tokens (RFC 8705) so a stolen bearer token can't be replayed from another client. The cost is operational: issuing, distributing, rotating, and revoking client certs at scale, usually handled by a service mesh or internal PKI rather than by hand.
fs: mTLS authenticates both client and server via X.509 certs at the transport layer; strong S2S/zero-trust identity.
fj: HTTPS already authenticates both sides — TLS does that. And I'd just rotate certs manually when they expire.

followups.q: How do certificate-bound tokens (RFC 8705) stop a stolen bearer token from being replayed?
followups.a: The access token is cryptographically bound to the client's mTLS certificate (a hash of the cert is embedded in the token). When the token is presented, the resource server checks it was sent over an mTLS connection using *that same* certificate. A thief with only the token but not the client's private key can't establish the matching mTLS connection, so the stolen token is useless elsewhere.

followups.q: Why is mTLS revocation operationally hard, and how is it handled at scale?
followups.a: X.509 revocation via CRLs (large, stale lists) or OCSP (a lookup per handshake, latency + availability risk) is clunky at fleet scale. The common answer is *short-lived certs*: issue certs valid for hours via an internal CA / mesh (SPIFFE/Istio), so you 'revoke' by not reissuing. Short lifetimes sidestep revocation infrastructure, which is why meshes automate rotation.

followups.q: Where does mTLS break down with TLS-terminating load balancers or CDNs?
followups.a: If a load balancer or CDN terminates TLS, the backend never sees the client cert — the mTLS identity stops at the edge. Either terminate mTLS *at* the backend (passthrough), or have the edge verify the cert and forward its identity in a trusted header (`X-Client-Cert`) protected from spoofing. Naive TLS termination at a proxy silently discards the client identity mTLS was meant to prove.

q: How should an API return structured errors, and what does RFC 9457 (problem+json) standardize?
a: Return errors with the correct HTTP status code plus a machine-readable body, rather than a 200 with an ad-hoc error field. RFC 9457 (which obsoletes RFC 7807) defines the application/problem+json media type with five standard members: type (a URI identifying the problem class), title (short human summary), status (the HTTP code, advisory), detail (specifics for this occurrence), and instance (a URI for this particular error). You can add extension members like a request/correlation id, retry timing, or a per-field validation list. Using a stable type URI lets clients branch on error class without string-matching prose, and a consistent shape across endpoints makes error handling uniform.
fs: problem+json: type/title/status/detail/instance + extensions; branch on the stable type URI, not on prose.
fj: I return 200 with a free-text 'error' string in the body — clients can regex it if they need the detail.

followups.q: Why should clients branch on `type` (a URI) rather than `title` or the HTTP status?
followups.a: `title` is human-readable prose that may be reworded or localized, breaking any client that string-matches it. The HTTP status is too coarse — many distinct problems share `400`/`409`. The `type` URI is a *stable, unique identifier* for the problem class, so clients can switch on it reliably across versions and languages. It's the contract; `title`/`detail` are for humans.

followups.q: What's the difference between the `type` and `instance` members?
followups.a: `type` identifies the *class* of problem (`/probs/out-of-credit`) and is shared by every occurrence of that error. `instance` identifies *this specific occurrence* (`/orders/abc/errors/123`), useful for support correlation and dereferencing details about that one failure. Think `type` = the error category clients branch on, `instance` = the unique pointer to this exact incident.

followups.q: Why does RFC 9457 call the `status` member 'advisory' if the HTTP status is already set?
followups.a: The HTTP status line is authoritative; the body's `status` just mirrors it so the problem document is self-contained when copied into logs or relayed through tools that lose the HTTP context. If they ever disagree, the actual HTTP status wins — duplicating it in the body is a convenience, not a second source of truth, which is why the spec marks it advisory.

q: Explain ETags and conditional requests. How do they save bandwidth and prevent lost updates?
a: An ETag is an opaque validator (a hash or version) the server returns with a representation. On a later GET the client sends If-None-Match: <etag>; if the resource is unchanged the server replies 304 Not Modified with no body, so the client reuses its cache and saves bandwidth. ETags also enable optimistic concurrency on writes: the client sends If-Match: <etag> on a PUT/PATCH, and if the resource changed underneath it the server returns 412 Precondition Failed instead of silently overwriting — preventing the lost-update problem. Strong ETags must match byte-for-byte; weak ETags (W/) allow semantically-equivalent variations. Pair ETags with Cache-Control to control both freshness and revalidation.
fs: ETag + If-None-Match -> 304 (cheap revalidation); If-Match -> 412 (optimistic concurrency, no lost update).
fj: Concurrent PUTs are rare enough in practice — I don't bother with If-Match and just let last write win.

followups.q: When would you use a weak ETag (W/) instead of a strong one?
followups.a: Use a weak ETag when two representations are *semantically* equivalent but not byte-identical — same data with different whitespace, gzip vs identity encoding, or a regenerated-but-equal page. Weak ETags suit cache revalidation (`If-None-Match`) but not byte-range requests or strict concurrency, which need the strong byte-exact guarantee. Strong = exact bytes; weak = same meaning.

followups.q: How does If-Match-based optimistic concurrency compare to pessimistic locking?
followups.a: Optimistic concurrency assumes conflicts are rare: no lock is held, you detect a clash at write time via `If-Match` and `412`, then retry — great for high read-to-write ratios. Pessimistic locking holds a lock throughout, serializing access and risking deadlocks but wasting no work. ETags fit HTTP's stateless model since there's no server-held lock between read and write.

followups.q: What's the failure mode if If-None-Match is omitted but Cache-Control max-age is set?
followups.a: While the response is still fresh (within `max-age`), the cache serves it *without revalidating*, so the client may see stale data and never sends `If-None-Match`. Conditional requests only kick in once the entry is stale. If you need every read revalidated for correctness, use `no-cache` (which forces revalidation via the ETag) rather than relying on `max-age` alone.

q: How do Cache-Control directives control HTTP caching for an API?
a: Cache-Control governs who may cache a response and for how long. max-age=N sets the freshness lifetime in seconds; while fresh, caches serve without contacting the origin. public allows shared (CDN/proxy) caching, private restricts it to the end-user's browser. no-cache means a cache may store the response but must revalidate (e.g. via ETag) before reuse, while no-store forbids storing it at all — use it for sensitive data. s-maxage overrides max-age for shared caches, and stale-while-revalidate lets a cache serve a slightly stale copy while refreshing in the background. For per-user API responses, private with a short max-age plus ETag revalidation is a common, safe combination.
fs: max-age sets freshness; public/private scope; no-cache=revalidate, no-store=never store; s-maxage for CDNs.
fj: no-cache and no-store both prevent caching — they're interchangeable. And marking responses public makes them cacheable everywhere, which speeds things up.

followups.q: Why is marking per-user API data `public` a serious bug, not just a tuning mistake?
followups.a: `public` lets *shared* caches (CDNs, proxies) store the response and serve it to *other* users — so user A's account data can be returned to user B. It's a data-leak vulnerability. Per-user responses must be `private` (browser-only) or `no-store`. The `public`/`private` directive isn't about performance here; it's an access-control boundary on who may hold a cached copy.

followups.q: How does stale-while-revalidate improve latency, and what's the trade-off?
followups.a: It lets a cache serve an expired copy *immediately* while fetching a fresh one in the background, so users never wait on revalidation — great for high-traffic, stale-tolerant data. The trade-off is users may briefly see stale content (up to the SWR window), so it's wrong for data that must be exact (balances, inventory) but ideal for feeds or config that can lag a few seconds.

followups.q: Why might you set both `s-maxage` and `max-age` to different values?
followups.a: `s-maxage` applies only to shared caches (CDN/proxy), overriding `max-age` there, while `max-age` governs the private browser cache. You'd give a CDN a long `s-maxage` (cache aggressively at the edge, you can purge it) but a short `max-age` so browsers revalidate sooner (you can't purge browser caches). It tunes edge and client caching independently for the same response.

q: How does a client-side circuit breaker work, and why pair it with retries?
a: A circuit breaker wraps calls to a dependency and tracks failures. In CLOSED state calls flow normally while it counts errors/timeouts; once failures cross a threshold it trips to OPEN and fails fast for a cooldown window, returning immediately (or a fallback) instead of piling up calls on a struggling service. After the cooldown it moves to HALF-OPEN, allowing a few trial requests: success closes the circuit, failure re-opens it. Pair it with retries-plus-backoff because retries alone amplify load on a failing dependency and can cause a retry storm; the breaker is the stop valve that prevents cascading failure and gives the downstream time to recover. Tune thresholds and cooldown to your dependency's real failure profile.
fs: CLOSED->OPEN (fail fast) on failure threshold->HALF-OPEN trial->close/reopen; stops retry storms cascading.
fj: Retries are enough on their own — just keep retrying with backoff until the dependency recovers.

followups.q: Why is the HALF-OPEN state necessary instead of just closing after the cooldown?
followups.a: Closing blindly after cooldown would dump full traffic onto a dependency that may still be down, immediately re-overwhelming it. HALF-OPEN lets through only a few probe requests to *test* recovery — success closes the circuit, failure re-opens it for another cooldown. It's a controlled, low-risk health check that avoids the thundering-herd of resuming full load on an unverified service.

followups.q: What should the breaker do for callers while it's OPEN — error or fallback?
followups.a: If a sensible fallback exists (cached/stale data, a default, a degraded feature), return it so the user experience degrades gracefully rather than erroring. If no fallback makes sense, fail fast with a clear error so the caller doesn't hang. The point of OPEN is to respond *immediately* either way — never to queue or attempt the doomed call — protecting both the caller and the downstream.

followups.q: How do you avoid a circuit breaker tripping on a localized blip across a fleet?
followups.a: Count failures over a rolling window with a minimum request volume so a couple of stray errors don't trip it, and trip on a *failure rate* (e.g. >50% of ≥20 requests) rather than a raw count. Per-instance breakers also localize the decision so one bad node doesn't open everyone's circuit. Tuning window, volume threshold, and rate to the dependency's real noise floor prevents false trips.

q: What is CORS, what triggers a preflight, and why can't it protect your API?
a: CORS is a browser security mechanism: by default the same-origin policy blocks a page on origin A from reading responses from origin B, and CORS lets the server opt in via response headers like Access-Control-Allow-Origin. For 'non-simple' requests (custom headers, methods beyond GET/POST/HEAD, or certain content types) the browser first sends an OPTIONS preflight; the server answers with Allow-Origin/Allow-Methods/Allow-Headers and a Max-Age to cache the decision. Critically, CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server, and only governs cross-origin reads from web pages — it is not authentication or authorization. Non-browser clients (curl, mobile, server-to-server) ignore it entirely, so you still need real auth on the API. Avoid reflecting arbitrary origins or using a wildcard with credentials.
fs: CORS = browser opt-in for cross-origin reads; OPTIONS preflight for non-simple requests; not auth, not server-enforced.
fj: CORS protects the API from unauthorized cross-origin requests — so I set it to reflect any Origin to fix CORS errors and let all clients through.

followups.q: Exactly what makes a request 'simple' so it skips the preflight?
followups.a: It must use `GET`, `HEAD`, or `POST`; carry only CORS-safelisted headers; and have a `Content-Type` of `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`, or `text/plain`. Add a custom header (`Authorization`, `X-Trace`) or send `application/json` and it becomes non-simple, firing an `OPTIONS` preflight. This is why JSON APIs with auth headers almost always preflight.

followups.q: Why is `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with credentials forbidden by browsers?
followups.a: Allowing *any* origin to send authenticated (cookie-bearing) cross-origin requests and read the response would let any malicious site act as the logged-in user. So browsers reject the combination of wildcard origin and `Allow-Credentials: true` — you must echo a *specific* origin.
